name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
لَآ إِكْرَاهَ فِى ٱلدِّينِ ۖ قَد تَّبَيَّنَ ٱلرُّشْدُ مِنَ ٱلْغَىِّ ۚ فَمَن يَكْفُرْ بِٱلطَّـٰغُوتِ وَيُؤْمِنۢ بِٱللَّهِ فَقَدِ ٱسْتَمْسَكَ بِٱلْعُرْوَةِ ٱلْوُثْقَىٰ لَا ٱنفِصَامَ لَهَا ۗ وَٱللَّهُ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ (٢٥٦)
```

```english
There is no compulsion in religion: rectitude has become distinct from error. So one who disavows fake deities and has faith in Allah has held fast to the firmest handle for which there is no breaking; and Allah is all-hearing, all-knowing. (256)
```

```arabic
ٱللَّهُ وَلِىُّ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ يُخْرِجُهُم مِّنَ ٱلظُّلُمَـٰتِ إِلَى ٱلنُّورِ ۖ وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ أَوْلِيَآؤُهُمُ ٱلطَّـٰغُوتُ يُخْرِجُونَهُم مِّنَ ٱلنُّورِ إِلَى ٱلظُّلُمَـٰتِ ۗ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلنَّارِ ۖ هُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ (٢٥٧)
```

```english
Allah is the wali of the faithful: He brings them out of darkness into light. As for the faithless, their awliya are the fake deities, who drive them out of light into darkness. They shall be the inmates of the Fire, and they will remain in it [forever]. (257)
```

## (Commentary)

**His saying, the Exalted:** لا إِكراه في الدين قد تبين الرشد من الغي **"There is no compulsion in religion: rectitude has become distinct from error."** (2:256). Compulsion (الإكراه) is to force or coerce someone into an act without their consent. Rectitude (الرشد) — with a *damma* on the first letter or on both the first and second — refers to attaining the correct objective of a matter and finding the highway of the path; its opposite is error (الغي). Thus, these two terms are broader than guidance (الهدى) and straying (الضلال), for the latter two refer to finding the path that leads to the destination or failing to do so, as has been said. It appears that the use of rectitude (الرشد) to mean finding the highway of the path is a case of applying a concept to its prime instance (المصداق); for the traveler, attaining the correct objective of the matter is to take the highway and the even way. Thus, his sticking to the path is one of the instances of attaining the correct objective. The truth is that the meanings of rectitude (الرشد) and guidance (الهدى) are distinct, though one may be applied to the instances of the other through a specific consideration, which is evident. The Exalted says: فَإِنْ آنَسْتُم مِّنْهُمْ رُشْدًا **"then if you perceive in them any rectitude"** (4:6), and He says: وَلَقَدْ آتَيْنَا إِبْرَاهِيمَ رُشْدَهُ مِن قَبْلُ **"Certainly We had given Abraham his rectitude before"** (21:51). The same applies to error (الغي) and straying (الضلال). For this reason, we mentioned previously that straying (الضلال) is to deviate from the path while remembering the goal and destination, whereas error (الغي) is to deviate while forgetting the goal, such that the person in error (الغوي) does not know what he wants or what he intends.

In His saying, the Exalted: لا اكراه في الدين **"There is no compulsion in religion,"** there is a negation of forced religion. This is because religion (الدين) is a chain of theoretical cognitions followed by practical ones, all unified by being beliefs (اعتقادات). Belief (الاعتقاد) and faith (الايمان) are among the matters of the heart (الامور القلبية) in which compulsion and coercion have no dominion. Compulsion only influences outward actions (الاعمال الظاهرية) and physical, material movements. As for heart-felt belief, it has other causes and heart-felt reasons of the same nature as belief and perception. It is impossible for ignorance to produce knowledge, or for non-scientific premises to give birth to a scientific assent (تصديقاً علمياً). Therefore, His saying: لا اكراه في الدين **"There is no compulsion in religion,"** if it is an informative proposition (قضية اخبارية) reporting on the state of the ontological creation (التكوين), it yields a religious ruling negating compulsion in religion and belief. If it is a legislative, prescriptive ruling (حكم انشائي تشريعي) — as evidenced by what the Exalted followed it with: قد تبين الرشد من الغي **"rectitude has become distinct from error"** — then it is a prohibition against forcing belief and faith. This prohibition rests upon an ontological reality (حقيقة تكوينية), which is what was previously explained: that compulsion only works and takes effect in the stage of physical actions, not heart-felt beliefs.

The Exalted has explained this ruling with His saying: قد تبين الرشد من الغي **"rectitude has become distinct from error,"** which serves as the rationale (التعليل). For compulsion and coercion are only resorted to by a wise commander or a rational educator in important matters where there is no way to explain the truth of the matter due to the simplicity of the subject's understanding or the dullness of the subordinate's mind, or for other reasons. In such cases, the ruler causes his command to be carried out through compulsion or by ordering blind imitation (التقليد) and the like. However, in important matters where the aspects of good and evil have been made clear, and the nature of the reward or punishment following their performance or omission has been established, there is no need for compulsion. Rather, it is for the human being to choose for himself what he wishes between the two sides of the action and the two outcomes of reward and punishment. Since the realities of religion have been uncovered and its path has been clarified through divine explanations made manifest by the Prophetic Sunnah, it has become clear that religion is rectitude (رشد) and that rectitude lies in following it, while error (الغي) lies in abandoning it and turning away from it. Based on this, there is no reason for anyone to compel another toward religion.

This is one of the verses indicating that Islam was not built upon the sword and blood, and did not decree compulsion and force, contrary to what has been claimed by several researchers — both those who affiliate themselves with Islam and others — that Islam is a religion of the sword. They argued this based on Jihad (الجهاد) — *the striving in the way of Allah* — which is one of the pillars of this religion.

The answer to this has already been provided within the discussion on the verses of fighting (القتال). We mentioned there that the fighting to which Islam invited is not for the purpose of achieving dominance or spreading religion by force and compulsion, but rather to revive the truth and defend the most precious possession of the primordial nature (الفطرة) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being* — which is monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts*. As for after monotheism has spread among the people and they have submitted to the religion of Prophethood, even if by remaining Jews or Christians, there is no conflict or dispute for a Muslim with a monotheist. Thus, the objection arises from a lack of contemplation.

It appears from what has preceded that the verse — meaning His saying: لا إِكراه في الدين **"There is no compulsion in religion"** — is not abrogated (غير منسوخة) by the "verse of the sword," as mentioned by some.

Among the evidences that the verse is not abrogated is the rationale (التعليل) contained within it, namely His saying: قد تبين الرشد من الغي **"rectitude has become distinct from error."** For an abrogating text (الناسخ) does not abrogate the ruling itself unless it abrogates the cause (علة) of the ruling; a ruling remains as long as its cause remains. It is well-known that the clarity of rectitude from error in the matter of Islam is something that cannot be removed by something like the verse of the sword. For His saying: **"kill the polytheists wherever you find them"** (9:5), for example, or His saying: **"And fight in the way of Allah"** (2:190), do not affect the manifestation of the truth of the religion in any way such that they would abrogate a ruling that is an effect (معلول) of this manifestation.

In other words, the verse justifies His saying: لا اكراه في الدين **"There is no compulsion in religion"** by the manifestation of the truth. This is a meaning whose state does not change before the revelation of the ruling of fighting or after its revelation; it is fixed in every case, and thus it is not abrogated.

**His saying, the Exalted:** فمن يكفر بالطاغوت ويؤمن بالله فقد استمسك بالعروة الوثقى **"So one who disbelieves in the Rebel and believes in Allah has certainly grasped the firmest handle"** (2:256). The Rebel (الطاغوت) refers to rebellion (الطغيان) and exceeding the limit; the word carries an intensive meaning, similar to the Kingdom (الملكوت) and Omnipotence (الجبروت). It is used for that through which rebellion occurs, such as the various types of deities worshipped besides Allah, like idols, devils, jinn, the leaders of misguidance among humans, and every followed one whose following Allah, the Glorified, does not approve of. It is used equally for masculine, feminine, singular, dual, and plural.

Disbelief (الكفر) was mentioned before faith (الايمان) in His saying: فمن يكفر بالطاغوت ويؤمن بالله **"So one who disbelieves in the Rebel and believes in Allah,"** to correspond with the order suited to the action occurring in the result (الجزاء), namely "grasping the firmest handle." This is because grasping something only occurs by leaving everything else and taking hold of the handle; thus, there is a leaving and then a taking. Therefore, disbelief — which is a leaving — was mentioned before faith — which is a taking — to match that. Grasping (الاستمساك) is to take and hold firmly. The handle (العروة) is that by which a thing is held, like the handle of a bucket or a vessel. The term *‘urwah* (العروة) also refers to any plant that has a root and whose leaves do not fall. The root of the word refers to attachment; it is said: *‘arāhu* and *i‘tarāhu*, meaning he attached himself to it.

The speech — meaning His saying: فقد استمسك بالعروة الوثقى **"has certainly grasped the firmest handle"** — is constructed as a metaphor (استعارة) to indicate that faith, in relation to happiness, is like the handle of a vessel in relation to the vessel and its contents. Just as a grip is not a secure grip until one seizes the handle, likewise, true happiness (السعادة الحقيقية) does not become stable, nor can its attainment be hoped for, unless the human being believes in Allah and disbelieves in the Rebel (الطاغوت).

**His saying, the Exalted:** لا انفصام لها والله سميع عليم **"which shall not break; and Allah is all-hearing, all-knowing"** (2:256). Breaking (الانفصام) is to be severed or fractured. The sentence is in the position of a circumstantial clause (حال) for the "handle," emphasizing the meaning of the "firmest handle." Then He followed it with His saying: والله سميع عليم **"and Allah is all-hearing, all-knowing,"** because faith and disbelief are connected to the heart and the tongue.

**His saying, the Exalted:** الله ولي الذين آمنوا يخرجهم **"Allah is the Wali of the faithful: He brings them out..."** (2:257) until the end of the verse. A portion of the discussion regarding the meaning of His bringing them out from light into darkness has already passed. We explained there that this "bringing out" and similar meanings are real, ontological matters (امور حقيقية), not metaphorical (مجازية), contrary to what many commentators and other researchers imagined — that they are metaphorical meanings intended to refer to outward actions (الاعمال الظاهرية) consisting of physical movements and stillness, and the good or bad ends that result from them. According to them, light (النور), for example, is the true belief by which the darkness of ignorance, the confusion of doubt, and the agitation of the heart are removed; and light is the righteous deed (صالح العمل) insofar as its rectitude is clear and its effect on happiness is manifest, just as physical light possesses these qualities. Darkness (الظلمة) is ignorance in belief, doubt, suspicion, and evil deeds — all of this being by way of metaphor (استعارة). The "bringing out from darkness into light" attributed to Allah, like the "bringing out from light into darkness" attributed to the Rebel (الطاغوت), is these very deeds and beliefs. Thus, according to them, there is nothing behind these deeds and beliefs — neither an act from Allah or others, such as "bringing out," nor an effect of the act of Allah or others, such as "light" or "darkness." This is what a group of commentators and researchers mentioned.

Others mentioned that Allah performs an act, such as bringing out from darkness into light, and giving life, expansion, and mercy, and what resembles them; and that effects result from His act, such as light, darkness, spirit, mercy, and the descent of angels, which our understandings do not reach and our senses cannot encompass. However, we believe in accordance with what Allah has informed — and He speaks the truth — that these things exist and that they are His acts, even if we do not encompass them with knowledge. The necessary consequence of this view, like the previous one, is that these words — meaning terms like light, darkness, bringing out, and the like — are used metaphorically as metaphors (استعارة). The only difference between the two views is that the instances (مصاديق) of light and darkness and the like, according to the first view, are our very deeds and beliefs, while according to the second view, they are matters external to our deeds and beliefs that we have no way of understanding, nor any path to attaining or perceiving.

Both views deviate from the straight path (صراط الاستقامة), like the one who exceeds the limit and the one who falls short. The truth in this matter is that these things which Allah, the Glorified, informed of bringing into existence and performing upon obedience or disobedience are real, ontological matters (امور حقيقية واقعية) without metaphor; however, they do not separate from our deeds and beliefs, but are rather their necessary concomitants (لوازمها) that reside within their inner reality (باطنها). The discussion on this has already passed. This does not contradict the fact that His saying, the Exalted: يخرجهم من الظلمات الى النور **"He brings them out from darkness into light,"** and His saying, the Exalted: يخرجونهم من النور الى الظلمات **"they bring them out from light into darkness,"** are two metonyms (كنايتين) for the guidance (هداية) of Allah, the Glorified, and the misguidance (إضلال) of the Rebel (الطاغوت). This is because, as mentioned in the discussion on speech, the dispute is in two positions: first, whether light, darkness, and the like possess a reality (حقيقة) in this world or are merely a comparison with no reality; and second, assuming we concede they have realities and ontological facts, is the use of the word — like "light," for example — for the reality that is the reality of guidance a literal (حقيقة) or metaphorical (مجاز) use? In any case, the two sentences — meaning His saying, the Exalted: يخرجهم من الظلمات الى النور **"He brings them out from darkness into light,"** and His saying, the Exalted: يخرجونهم من النور الى الظلمات **"they bring them out from light into darkness"** — are metonyms for guidance and misguidance. Otherwise, it would necessitate that both the believer and the disbeliever possess both light and darkness together. For the necessity of bringing the believer out from darkness into light is that he was in darkness before faith, and vice versa for the disbeliever. Thus, all believers and disbelievers — those who lived only as believers or only as disbelievers — when they reach the age of religious obligation (التكليف), if they believe, they come out from darkness into light, and if they disbelieve, they come out from light into darkness. So before that, they would be in both light and darkness together, and this is as you see [untenable].

However, it can be said: The human being, according to his creation, is upon the light of the primordial nature (نور الفطرة), which is a general light that accepts detailed elaboration. As for true cognitions and righteous deeds in detail, he is still in darkness because his matter has not yet become distinct. Light and darkness in this sense do not contradict each other, nor is their coexistence impossible. The believer, through his faith, comes out from this darkness into the light of cognitions and acts of obedience in detail. The disbeliever, through his disbelief, comes out from the light of the primordial nature into the darknesses of disbelief and detailed acts of disobedience. The use of "light" (النور) in the singular and "darknesses" (الظلمات) in the plural in His saying, the Exalted: يخرجهم من الظلمات الى النور **"He brings them out from darkness into light,"** and His saying, the Exalted: يخرجونهم من النور الى الظلمات **"they bring them out from light into darkness,"** is to indicate that the truth is one and there is no difference within it, just as falsehood is scattered and diverse, possessing no unity. The Exalted says: وَأَنَّ هَٰذَا صِرَاطِي مُسْتَقِيمًا فَاتَّبِعُوهُ وَلَا تَتَّبِعُوا السُّبُلَ فَتَفَرَّقَ بِكُمْ **"This is My straight path, so follow it, and do not follow [other] ways, for they will separate you..."** (6:153).

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, it is narrated by Abu Dawud, al-Nasa'i, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, al-Nahhas in his *Nasikh*, Ibn Mandah in *Ghara'ib Shu'bah*, Ibn Hibban, Ibn Mardawayh, al-Bayhaqi in his *Sunan*, and al-Diya' in *al-Mukhtarah*, from Ibn Abbas, who said: "A woman from the Ansar would be such that her children would not survive (مقلاة). So she would make a vow upon herself that if a child of hers lived, she would make him a Jew. When the Banu al-Nadir were exiled, there were among them sons of the Ansar. They said: 'We will not leave our sons.' So Allah revealed: لا إِكراه في الدين **'There is no compulsion in religion.'**"

**I say:** This meaning has also been narrated through other chains from Sa'id ibn Jubayr and from al-Sha'bi.

In the same source: Abd ibn Humayd, Ibn Jarir, and Ibn al-Mundhir narrated from Mujahid, who said: "The [Banu] al-Nadir had nursed men from the [tribe of] Aws. When the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم ordered their exile, their sons from the Aws said: 'We will certainly go with them and we will certainly follow their religion.' Their families prevented them and compelled them toward Islam. So this verse was revealed concerning them: لا إِكراه في الدين **'There is no compulsion in religion.'**"

**I say:** This meaning is also narrated through a different chain, and it does not contradict what preceded regarding the vow of the women whose children did not survive to make them Jews.

Also in the same source: Ibn Ishaq and Ibn Jarir narrated from Ibn Abbas: Regarding His saying: لا إِكراه في الدين **"There is no compulsion in religion,"** he said: "It was revealed concerning a man from the Ansar from the Banu Salim ibn Awf called al-Husayn. He had two sons who were Christians, while he was a Muslim man. He said to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم: 'Should I not compel them, for they have refused everything except Christianity?' So Allah revealed that concerning him."

In *al-Kafi*, narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): He said: "The light (النور) is the Progeny of Muhammad (آل محمد), and the darknesses (الظلمات) are their enemies."

**I say:** This is of the nature of "application" (الجري) or from the perspective of the inner meaning (الباطن) or esoteric interpretation (التأويل).
